title: Chest Tube and Closed Chest Drainage Care
subtitle: The nurse manages a closed chest drainage system — keeping it upright below the chest, recognizing normal tidaling versus an air leak, never routinely clamping, and responding when the tube disconnects or is dislodged.

patient.name: Chest Tube Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse cares for a patient with a chest tube and closed chest drainage system.

labs.v: Place tube end in sterile water.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a chest tube connected to a closed drainage system, which removes air, fluid, or blood from the pleural space and helps the lung re-expand. The system is kept upright and below the level of the chest so gravity aids drainage, and the tubing is kept free of kinks and dependent loops. In the water-seal chamber, tidaling (fluctuation with breathing) shows the tube is patent and stops when the lung re-expands or the tube is blocked; brief bubbling when the patient coughs is expected with a pneumothorax, but continuous bubbling suggests an air leak and the connections are checked. Suction-control bubbling should be gentle. The nurse does not routinely clamp, strip, or milk the tube, since clamping can cause a tension pneumothorax. Sterile water and an occlusive dressing are kept at the bedside: if the tube disconnects from the system, the end is placed in sterile water to restore the seal, and if the tube is pulled out of the chest, the site is covered with a sterile occlusive dressing taped on three sides and the provider is notified. Drainage over 100 mL/hr, sudden bright-red output, increasing shortness of breath, or crepitus is reported.
providerOrders: Keep the drainage system upright and below the level of the chest
Monitor the water seal for tidaling and report continuous bubbling
Keep tubing free of kinks; do not routinely clamp, strip, or milk it
Keep sterile water and an occlusive dressing at the bedside
Report drainage over 100 mL/hr, bright-red output, or respiratory distress

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about chest drainage care?

opts.text: A chest tube drains air or fluid to re-expand the lung
The drainage system is kept upright and below the chest
Tidaling in the water-seal chamber shows the tube is patent
The tubing should hang in dependent loops below the bed
Continuous bubbling in the water seal can mean an air leak
Routine clamping of the chest tube is recommended
Keeping the system below the chest lets gravity drain fluid
Tidaling stops when the lung re-expands or the tube is blocked
Continuous water-seal bubbling means air is entering the system
Clamping the tube during transport prevents tension pneumothorax
Sudden bright-red drainage over 100 mL/hr should be reported
Crepitus around the site should be assessed and noted

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: The tube disconnects from the drainage system

opts.text: Submerge the tube's end in sterile water to restore a seal
Assess respiratory status and oxygen saturation
Obtain a new sterile drainage system
Reconnect the tube and resume suction as ordered
Notify the provider and document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Expected finding or notify the provider?

opts.text: Gentle tidaling in the water-seal chamber
Continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber
Brief bubbling in the water seal when the patient coughs
Drainage decreasing and changing from red to serous
Sudden bright-red drainage over 100 mL/hr
Crepitus spreading around the insertion site
Tubing free of kinks with the system upright
Sudden loss of tidaling with new shortness of breath

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: The chest tube is accidentally pulled out of the chest
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Cover the site immediately with a sterile dressing
Tape the occlusive dressing on three sides
Assess respiratory status and oxygen saturation
Notify the provider immediately
Stay with the patient and document
Keeping the drainage system upright and below the chest
Coiling the tubing in dependent loops below the bed
Reporting continuous bubbling in the water seal
Routinely clamping the chest tube during ambulation
Keeping sterile water and an occlusive dressing at bedside
Stripping or milking the tube routinely
Encouraging deep breathing and incentive spirometry
Raising the system above the chest to read it

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
